coredata.dc:title: Poly(dimethyl siloxane)-based copolymer dielectric composites with copper sulfide loaded carbon nanotubes doped with carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Ying-Jie
Wang, Jing-Wen
Zhang, Yang
Zhang, Zi-Long
Zhang, Ming-Yue
Tao, Liang
coredata.dc:description: The renewal of electromechanical transducers is tightly linked to the ongoing development of elastomers with excellent dielectric properties, but this positive progress is often plagued by the dilemma of the lower dielectric constant of the elastomers themselves. In this work, carbon nanotubes loaded with copper sulfide and doped with carbon quantum dots (abbreviated as CD-CuS@CNT) were successfully fabricated through hydrothermal reaction, followed by the incorporation of this filler into poly(dimethyl siloxane)-based copolymers (H-PDMS for short) to form percolation composites, additionally co-blended composite films of H-PDMS and carbon nanotubes loaded with copper sulfide were prepared as a control. Results exhibit that the permittivity of 2.33 vol% CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composites is 53.34 at 100 Hz, which is 1.3 and 19.2 times that of 2.12 vol% CuS@CNT/H-PDMS and pure H-PDMS, while the loss is only 0.05. Moreover, the investigation revealed the role of carbon quantum dots in the whole system to enhance the dielectric properties of the composites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer-matrix composites
Carbon quantum dots
Carbon nanotubes
Dielectric properties

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52273247). Special thanks are extended to the Center for Microscopy and Analysis of Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics for partial testing of the data including XRD and SEM.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The fabrication route of composite films. (b) Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of CD-CuS@CNT.
(a) FT-IR spectra of CNT, CuS, CuS@CNT and CD-CuS@CNT. (b) Raman spectra of CNT, CuS@CNT and CD-CuS@CNT. XPS spectra of CD-CuS@CNT: (c) Survey scan, (d) C 1 s, (e) S 2p and (f) O 1 s.

(a) XRD spectra of CNT, CuS, CuS@CNT and CD-CuS@CNT. Normal distribution of particle size of copper sulfide in CuS@CNT (b) and CD-CuS@CNT (c). SEM images of CuS@CNT (d, e) and CD-CuS@CNT (f, g). TEM images of CD-CuS@CNT (h, i).

DC conductivity of CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS (a) and CuS@CNT/H-PDMS (b) composites as a function of filler volume fraction. Dependence of dielectric constant (c) and dielectric loss (d) on the frequency of CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composites at room temperature with different volume fractions of fillers. Dependence of dielectric constant (e) and dielectric loss (f) on the frequency of CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composites at room temperature with different volume fractions of fillers. (g) Dielectric constant of CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS and CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composites with different volume fractions of fillers at 100 Hz. (h) Logarithmic plots of global loss factor log
ε
"
versus frequency log f of CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS with different filler volume loading. (i) Comparison of dielectric constant and loss (100 Hz) of different PDMS-based nanocomposites.

(a) Histogram of the content of carbon quantum dots in the filler versus the molar ratio of the raw material, where the content of carbon quantum dots in the CD-CuS@CNT filler is calculated from the difference between the suspension of the filler prepared through hydrothermal reaction and the residual amount of carbon quantum dots in the filtered filtrate, with samples of different molar ratios being repeated five times and averaged. Variation of dielectric constant (b) and dielectric loss (c) with frequency for 2.33 vol% CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composite films, where the carbon quantum dot content in the filler varies. (d) Variation of carbon quantum dot content at different frequencies on the change of dielectric constant. (e) Variation of the dielectric constant with frequency for 2.33 vol% CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composite films at different temperatures. (f) Nyquist plots of CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composite films at different filler contents, where the circuit is an equivalent circuit for the composite film.

Dielectric strength and shape parameter of CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS (a) and CuS@CNT/H-PDMS (b) composites with different volume fractions of fillers. Weibull distribution of dielectric strength of CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS (c) and CuS@CNT/H-PDMS (d) composites.

(a) Schematic illustration of the equivalent micro-capacitor of CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composite and the distribution of micro-capacitors with different filler contents. (b) Simulation of the edge electric field distribution of a micro-capacitor in H-PDMS. Simulation of the electric field distribution (c) and electric field flow lines (d) of the dielectric inside a micro-capacitor.

head.ce:title: Poly(dimethyl siloxane)-based copolymer dielectric composites with copper sulfide loaded carbon nanotubes doped with carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Technology, Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Nanjing 210016, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Technology
Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Technology, Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Nanjing 210016, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein we used copper sulfide loaded carbon nanotubes and poly(dimethyl siloxane)-based copolymers to prepare composite films, while doping the filler with carbon quantum dots to strengthen the dielectric constant and curb the dielectric loss. The composite prepared with a filler content of 2.33 vol% has a dielectric constant of 53.34 and a dielectric loss of 0.05 at 100 Hz and room temperature.

The renewal of electromechanical transducers is tightly linked to the ongoing development of elastomers with excellent dielectric properties, but this positive progress is often plagued by the dilemma of the lower dielectric constant of the elastomers themselves. In this work, carbon nanotubes loaded with copper sulfide and doped with carbon quantum dots (abbreviated as CD-CuS@CNT) were successfully fabricated through hydrothermal reaction, followed by the incorporation of this filler into poly(dimethyl siloxane)-based copolymers (H-PDMS for short) to form percolation composites, additionally co-blended composite films of H-PDMS and carbon nanotubes loaded with copper sulfide were prepared as a control. Results exhibit that the permittivity of 2.33 vol% CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composites is 53.34 at 100 Hz, which is 1.3 and 19.2 times that of 2.12 vol% CuS@CNT/H-PDMS and pure H-PDMS, while the loss is only 0.05. Moreover, the investigation revealed the role of carbon quantum dots in the whole system to enhance the dielectric properties of the composites.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: From energy storage devices such as high energy capacitors to electromechanical transducers such as generators and sensors, dielectric elastomers, a kind of electroactive polymers, have been in constant demand for high-power electronic devices. Poly(dimethyl siloxane) (PDMS), with its biocompatibility[1] and low viscosity loss[2], is one of the broadly accepted candidates for dielectric elastomers in strain sensors[3], electromagnetic shielding materials[4], and actuators[5]. However, the lower dielectric constant, a key factor affecting the actuation performance of the material[6], is holding back the development of PDMS. A number of approaches have been proposed to address the problem of low dielectric constant in PDMS. The dielectric constant reflects the polarization rate of the material, so one strategy is to graft polar groups into PDMS to increase its polarization strength, but this approach leads to an increase in the glass transition temperature of PDMS, which diminishes the ductility of the elastic deformation of PDMS and a smaller rise in the dielectric constant. Another strategy is to dope PDMS with a second phase, which is generally divided into ceramic fillers and conductive fillers. Ceramic fillers such as BaTiO3 [7] and PbZrTiO3 [8] can boost the dielectric constant of PDMS by virtue of their high dielectric constant, but require the addition of a high-weight fraction of filler (normally above 50 wt%)[9], which causes a deterioration in the mechanical properties of PDMS. In contrast, conductive fillers (graphene[10], carbon nanotubes[11] and MXene[12] as well as others) can remedy this deficiency by giving a considerable boost to the dielectric constant of PDMS when the content is not above the percolation threshold (typically less than 10 vol%). A side effect of conductive fillers, nevertheless, is the increase in dielectric loss. Wei et al. [13]adopted KH560 and gas-phase treated SiO2 to modify treated graphene and added it to PDMS to construct a bimodal network. The obtained composites with a filler content of 1.32 vol% achieved a permittivity of 18.8 at 100 Hz with a loss of 0.13, chemically modifying the filler thereby alleviating the filler dispersion problem offers a viable pathway for the preparation of polymer-based dielectric nanocomposites with excellent dielectric properties.

In the long-standing research on conductive fillers, carbon nanotubes, with their excellent physicochemical properties[14–16], have been one of the desirable candidates for improving the dielectric properties of composites. In addition, transition metal sulfides such as CuS[17], as a semiconductor material, when compounded with carbon nanotubes can inhibit the generation of leakage currents in the composite and improve the dispersion of carbon nanotubes in the matrix. Carbon quantum dots (CD), a carbon based zero-dimensional material with a size of less than 10 nm, have a wide range of applications in areas such as optical imaging[18], drug delivery[19] and biosensors[20] due to their low toxicity[21], abundant surface-active functional groups[22] and unique quantum tunneling effect[23]. Besides, its high specific surface area and unique edge effect can effectively enhance the interfacial properties between CNT and CuS[24], enabling the composite filler to perform at an unprecedented level, which has rarely been explored in terms of improving dielectric properties.

In this work, the composite films were fabricated through solution casting, in which the matrix (referred to as H-PDMS) was obtained using a one-step condensation polymerization of bis(3-aminopropyl) terminated poly(dimethyl siloxane) and different isocyanate chain expanders, while carbon nanotubes loaded with copper sulfide were selected as the filler, and carbon quantum dots were doped into the filler (abbreviated as CD-CuS@CNT) to strengthen the dielectric properties of the composite. By studying the dielectric properties and elastic modulus of the composite, it can be found that the maximum dielectric strength of the composite after the addition of filler reaches 155.35 MV/m and the modulus of elasticity is below 3 MPa. The dielectric constant of 2.45 vol% CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS is 53.34 at 100 Hz and the loss is only 0.05. Compared to pure H-PDMS, the dielectric constant is increased by a factor of 19. Besides, the role played for carbon quantum dots in optimizing the dielectric properties of the composites is revealed.

Bis(3-aminopropyl) terminated poly(dimethyl siloxane) (H2N–PDMS–NH2, Mn = 1000), Copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O, 99.5 %), Sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate (Na2S2O3·5H2O, 99.5 %), isophorone diisocyanate (IPDI, 99.5 %), 4, 4′-methylenebis(isocyanato phenyl) (MDI, 99.5 %), Glucose (AR, 99.5 %), Ascorbic acid (AR, 99.5 %) and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5 %) were purchased from Nanjing Xinhua-yuan Chemical Co., LTD. Carbonylated carbon nanotubes (CNT, 99.5 %) with a diameter of 50–60 nm and a length of 15–20 μm were purchased from Shenzhen Nano port Co., LTD. The reagents were used without further purification.

After reading previous papers on the synthesis of silicone rubber[25–27], the elastomer was synthesized using the following method: DMF (15 mL), MDI (1 g, 0.4 mol) and IPDI (1.334 g, 0.6 mol) were in turn placed one by one to a three-necked round-bottom flask and stirred magnetically for 1 h (Fig. 1 a). Afterwards, H2N–PDMS–NH2 (10 g, 1 mol) was poured into it and stirred under nitrogen gas at 60 °C for 9 h. Subsequently, the obtained solution was placed into a PTFE mold tray and dried at 80 °C. After that, the H-PDMS film was made by vacuum drying at 80 °C for 12 h.

The preparation of filler was optimized on the basis of previous work[28,29], specifically as follows: 0.02 g of carbonylated carbon nanotubes, 0.1 g of CuSO4·5H2O, 0.0994 g of Na2S2O3·5H2O, an amount of glucose and ascorbic acid were added to 75 mL of distilled water and sonicated until they were well dispersed, where the molar ratio of glucose to ascorbic acid was 10:1. Notably, the molar ratios of the amount of glucose and ascorbic acid added to the amount of CuSO4·5H2O and Na2S2O3·5H2O were 1:1, 2:1, 4:1, 6:1, 8:1 and 10:1. The addition amounts for different molar ratios are shown in Table S1. The resulting mixture was placed in a 100 mL PTFE-lined stainless-steel autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 12 h to obtain CD-CuS@CNT composite (Fig. 1b). Similarly, CuS@CNT composite was prepared in the same procedure and used as a control, without the addition of glucose and ascorbic acid in the hydrothermal reaction.

In advance, 0.15 g of H-PDMS elastomer was added to 2 mL of DMF solution to dissolve it fully. Afterwards, a certain volume amount of CD-CuS@CNT filler (0 vol%, 0.76 vol%, 1.25 vol%, 1.48 vol%, 1.86 vol%, 2.12 vol%, 2.45 vol%, 2.67 vol% and 2.93 vol%) was placed in the H-PDMS solution and the suspension was fabricated using a physical co-mingling method, whereby three iterations of sonication for 1 h followed by magnetic stirring for 10 min were performed. The suspension was dropped into a PTFE mold tray and dried at 80 °C for 6 h at atmospheric pressure, then at 80 °C for 12 h under vacuum to obtain CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composite films. A series of CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composite films with different filler contents were obtained in the same way as a control group.

After measuring the carbon quantum dot content of a series of fillers prepared under different molar ratio conditions, it was found that as the content of glucose and ascorbic acid gradually increased, the final carbon quantum dot content in the filler rose, but when the molar ratio was 10:1, the carbon quantum dot content no longer increased. We therefore chose a formulation with a molar ratio of 8:1 for the preparation of the filler, which was fabricated with a maximum carbon quantum dot content of approximately 24.5 wt% and used this filler for subsequent further studies.

To demonstrate the successful preparation of CD-CuS@CNT fillers, FT-IR, Raman and XPS spectra were carried out. In Fig. 2 a, the diffraction peaks at 3444 and 1637 cm−1 correspond to –OH and C = O stretching vibrations of the carbon nanotubes[30], respectively, attesting to the presence of carboxyl groups on the carbon nanotubes. A set of characteristic diffraction peaks appearing at 1098–1019 cm−1 are caused by asymmetric stretching vibrations of the Cu-S bond, meanwhile the diffraction peak of 612 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of Cu-S in copper sulfide. This peak is found in both CuS@CNT and CD-CuS@CNT, verifying the loading of copper sulfide onto carbon nanotubes. Two typical peaks of carbon materials can be observed in the Raman spectra (Fig. 2b), the D peak and the G peak, respectively. The D peak originates from the structural disorder and defects of sp3 carbon atoms in carbon nanotubes[31], and the G peak originates from the first order scattering of E2g phonons from sp2 carbon atoms in carbon nanotubes[32]. The intensity ratio ID/IG of the D and G peaks can reflect the magnitude of the defects within the sp2 average unit area[33]. The test results show that the ID/IG values of the carbon nanotubes increase with the introduction of copper sulfide as well as carbon quantum dots, implying that the defects in the region of the sp2 structure are enhanced as the disorder of the carbon hybrid filler increases with more reactants during the hydrothermal reaction process. Besides, the XPS spectra of CD-CuS@CNT are shown in Fig. 2c, 2d, 2e and 2f, which can be observed from the full spectrum to contain three main elements (C, S and O). The C 1 s spectrum is divided into five peaks, of which the peaks at 288.3, 286.6, 285.9, 284.2 and 283.9 eV are from π-π* interacting carbon atoms, O-C = O, C-O, C-N and C–C. The five peaks in the S 2p spectrum correspond to Cu-S (168.5 eV), S2 2− 2p3/2 (163.9 eV), S2 2− 2p1/2 (163.1 eV), S2− 2p1/2 (161.8 eV) and S2− 2p3/2 (161.1 eV), where the 161.8 and 163.9 eV peaks confirm that the unsaturated S atoms in the presence of bridging S2 2− or apical S2− [34]. In addition, the O 1 s spectrum consists of three peaks at 533.5, 532.1 and 531 eV attributed to –COOH, C-O and C = O respectively, the presence of which is consistent with the FT-IR analysis and provides favorable evidence for the successful preparation of CD-CuS@CNT.

In the XRD patterns (Fig. 3 a), the characteristic diffraction peak at 26.1° represents the (002) crystalline planes of the carbon material, while the diffraction peaks at 27.66°, 29.28°, 31.78°, 32.86°, 47.96°, 52.72° and 59.34° correspond to the (101), (102), (103), (006), (110), (108) and (116) crystalline planes of CuS[35], respectively, indicating the successful preparation of CuS. The presence of these diffraction peaks in the XRD patterns of both CuS@CNT and CD-CuS@CNT demonstrates the successful loading of copper sulfide onto carbon nanotubes with good crystallinity. Furthermore, the diffraction peaks of the XRD patterns of CD-CuS@CNT are all attenuated, which may be caused by the introduction of carbon quantum dots with smaller grain sizes. The Debye-Scherrer formula[36] can be described as the following equation:
(1)
D =
K γ
B cos θ
where K is the Scherrer constant and takes the value of 0.89, D is the average thickness of the grain in the direction perpendicular to the grain plane, B is the half-height width of the diffraction peak of the experimental sample,
θ
is the Bragg angle and
γ
is the X-ray wavelength, with a typical value of 1.54056 Å for Cu kα.

Using equation (1) substituted for the full width of the half-peak of the strongest diffraction peak corresponding to the (103) crystal plane in the XRD spectra of CuS@CNT and CD-CuS@CNT respectively, the average grain size of CuS in CuS@CNT and CD-CuS@CNT can be calculated to be 366 nm and 228 nm, which are consistent with the grain size range of the maximum probability in the statistical distribution interval of grain sizes of CuS in Fig. 3b and 3c. The SEM images of CuS@CNT (Fig. 3d and 3e) and CD-CuS@CNT (Fig. 3f and 3g) reveal that the surface of the copper sulfide synthesized through the hydrothermal method is wrinkled and the copper sulfide is clearly separated from the carbon nanotubes with unsatisfactory apposition, but the copper sulfide adheres more tightly to the carbon nanotubes after the introduction of carbon quantum dots and presents a grapevine bunch-like structure. To further analyze the cause of this phenomenon, TEM images observed that the black dots formed in the middle of the elongated curved distribution of carbon nanotubes are carbon quantum dots, as shown in Fig. 3h and 3i. The particle size of CuS becomes smaller owing to the mutual attraction of the negatively charged CD and the positively charged CuS, prompting more CuS to grow on the CNT surface[37]. The large specific surface area of CD itself also offers more interfaces for charge accumulation for this functional filler [38], this creates a solid basis for improved dielectric properties.

After conducting conductivity tests on a range of composite films with different filler contents, it was noted that the conductivity of the composite films increased with increasing conductive filler content. Notably, there is a stepwise increase in the growth which can be explained using the percolation theory model[39]. The percolation thresholds of CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS and CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composites are 2.45 vol% and 2.29 vol% respectively (Fig. 4 a and 4b) using equations (2) and (3). The increase in percolation threshold can be explained by the fact that the introduced carbon quantum dots enhance the interfacial properties of the carbon nanotubes and copper sulfide nanospheres. The large specific surface area of the carbon quantum dots provides abundant nucleation sites for the copper sulfide to attach to the carbon nanotubes, resulting in a smaller size of the copper sulfide (the average particle size is reduced by about 200 nm), which inhibits charge transport and prevents the formation of a conductive network[40,41].
(2)
σ ∝
f c
- f
- s
f <
f c
(3)
σ ∝
f -
f c
t
f >
f c
where
σ
,
f c
and
f
represent the direct current conductivity, the percolation threshold and the volume fraction of filler in the composite, respectively, while s and t represent the threshold index.

The dielectric constant and dielectric loss of CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS (Fig. 4c and 4d) and CuS@CNT/H-PDMS (Fig. 4e and 4f) are shown in Fig. 4. The dielectric constant of pure H-PDMS is less subject to frequency because there is almost no free charge present[42]. Furthermore, the introduction of fillers favors inducing interfacial polarization in the composites, which, as a long-range polarization[43], is dependent on the frequency, with the polarization effect being significant at low frequencies and becoming weaker at high frequencies, an effect also intuitively reflected in the data. In contrast, the 2.33 vol% CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS (dielectric constant of 53.34 and loss of 0.05) has better dielectric properties than the 2.12 vol% CuS@CNT/H-PDMS (dielectric constant of 41.04 and loss of 0.09) at 100 Hz. Besides, it is interesting to note that the permittivity of the composite increases and then decreases with increasing filler content in Fig. 4g, which may be due to the following reasons: (1) Carbon nanotubes as the conductive layer, semiconductor copper sulfide and H-PDMS as the dielectric layer to form micro-capacitors[44], the more the number of micro-capacitors, the higher the capacitance of the material and the higher the dielectric constant. However, as the content of conductive filler gradually increases, the fillers easily aggregate with each other and thus form a conductive network (2.4 × 10−10 S/m and 5.7 × 10−4 S/m for CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS at 1.96 vol% and 2.85 vol% filler content respectively, a full six orders of magnitude increase in conductivity), and the generation of leakage currents as well as the reduction in the number of micro-capacitors leads to a decrease in the dielectric constant[45]. (2) Initially, there is a difference in conductivity between CD-CuS@CNT (3.6 × 106 S/m) and H-PDMS (2.1 × 10−11 S/m), and the interfacial polarization effect was enhanced, but as the filler content increased, this conductivity difference gradually compensates, resulting in a decrease in the permittivity[46]. Fig. 4h presents a frequency dependence plot of the loss factor of the CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composite films calculated using the Cole-Cole model, the equations used and the specific analysis are available in the Supporting Information. Overall, the composite films we have prepared have excellent dielectric properties compared to other similar work (Fig. 4i).

After systematically exploring the effect of filler content on the dielectric properties of the composite films, CD-CuS@CNT fillers with different carbon quantum dot contents were fabricated to examine the role of carbon quantum dots in enhancing the dielectric properties of the composites. The control of the different carbon quantum dot contents was determined depending on the different molar ratios of the amounts of glucose and ascorbic acid added to the amounts of CuSO4·5H2O and Na2S2O3·5H2O (Fig. 5 a), the different carbon quantum dot contents are abbreviated in terms of molar ratios in order to better investigate the rule. In addition, the filler content was fixed at 2.33 vol%, which was the filler content previously discussed for optimum dielectric properties. In Fig. 5b, the dielectric constant continues to increase as the carbon quantum dot content increases, and reaches a limiting value when the carbon quantum dot doping content reaches its maximum value. Meanwhile, in Fig. 5c, the dielectric loss gradually decreases with increasing carbon quantum dot content. This demonstrated that the carbon quantum dots could enhance the permittivity of the composite film and suppress the loss. A possible reason for this phenomenon is the large specific surface area of carbon quantum dots which brings an abundant interface for CD-CuS@CNT, providing more surface area for charge accumulation (the conductivity of CD-CuS@CNT /h-PDMS composites is higher than that of CuS@CNT/h-PDMS at the same filler content) and enhancing interfacial polarization[47].

To get a clearer understanding of the role of carbon quantum dots at different frequencies, we tested the dielectric constant of CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composite films with different carbon quantum dot contents at different frequencies (Fig. 5d). Results show that the filler with the highest carbon quantum dot content has the most noticeable improvement on the dielectric constant at low frequencies (102 Hz), which is 1.3 times higher overall than at high frequencies (106 Hz). Indicating that the carbon quantum dots are more frequency dependent and better at low frequencies to augment the dielectric properties. Temperature affects the dielectric properties of the material through influencing the polarization intensity in turn. To investigate the temperature dependence of the composite films, the dielectric constant of the 2.33 vol% CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composite films was tested at different temperatures (Fig. 5e). It was observed that the dielectric constant of the composite films decreased gradually with increasing temperature, because the high temperature intensified the thermal perturbation of the molecules within the material, which made it difficult for the molecules to orientate, rotate and aggregate, resulting in weaker dipole polarization and interfacial polarization leading to a decrease in the dielectric constant[48,49]. However, the dielectric constant does not change in magnitude and the experimental results reveal that the change in dielectric constant at low frequencies (102–103 Hz) is small when the temperature is increased from 25 °C to 250 °C, which provides a promising basis for the preparation of high temperature resistant actuators. In order to better investigate the charge transfer performance of the CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composite films, the Nyquist curves of the composite films with different filler contents were tested (Fig. 5f). It was found that the diameter of the half-circle of the Nyquist curve gradually decreased with the increase of the filler content, which indicated that the charge transfer resistance decreased and the conductive network was gradually formed in the films[50]. For a specific explanation of the equivalent circuit diagram of the CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composite film refer to the Supporting Information.

The dielectric strength and shape parameter of both CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS and CD-CuS@CNT composites increases and then decreases with increasing filler content, achieving a maximum value of 155.35 MV/m at 1.59 vol% CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS, which is 1.2 times higher than 1.48 vol% CuS@CNT/H-PDMS (Fig. 6 a and 6b). The reason for the enhanced dielectric strength can be deduced from the introduction of carbon quantum dots enhancing the π-π interactions between CuS and CNT [51,52] and reducing the physical entanglement between CNT, preventing the extension of electrical dendrites during electrical breakdown[53]. Meanwhile, a two-parameter Weibull distribution of the dielectric strength distribution states of the CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS and CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composites (Fig. 6c and 6d) was performed using Equation (4).
(4)
P
E
= 1 - exp
-
E
E b
β
in which P(E), E, Eb and β stand for the cumulative probability of electric breakdown, the dielectric strength measured in the experiment, the characteristic dielectric strength and the shape parameter, respectively[54]. It can be seen from Fig. 6c and 6d that the trend of the shape parameter is consistent with the dielectric strength. The magnitude of β reflects the dielectric reliability of the composite film, and its trend can be studied from another perspective of the breakdown stability of the composite. 1.56 vol% CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composite film has the largest shape parameters and the highest corresponding dielectric strength, which proves its high breakdown stability. Whereas the introduction of conductive fillers normally results in a decrease in the dielectric strength of the composite, in this work the dielectric strength was increased by a factor of 2.3, presumably for the following reasons: The SEM diagram illustrates that the presence of more small sized copper sulfide impedes the extension of the electrical dendrites, while the copper sulfide blocks and delays current breakdown[55].

Micro-capacitor theory can explain the increase in dielectric constant with the introduction of filler. In Fig. 7 a, the conductive layer and the H-PDMS form a micro-capacitor. The increase in filler causes the thickness of the dielectric layer of the micro-capacitor to decrease, increasing the capacitance, which in turn raises the dielectric constant. When the filler content approaches the percolation threshold, the thickness of the dielectric layer approaches the limiting minimum, at which point the conductive layers come into contact with each other, forming a conductive network and reducing the dielectric constant. In addition to the formation of the conductive network, the results of the finite element simulation reveal that adjacent micro-capacitors produce a fringe field effect[56] which becomes stronger as the number of micro-capacitors increases (Fig. 7b), thereby affecting the dielectric constant of the material. Besides, the presence of high dielectric polymers causes electric field distortion[57] inside the micro-capacitor (Fig. 7c and 7d), whereby the direction of the electric field changes because of the interference of the high dielectric constant polymer, which also increases with the number of micro-capacitors. Fringe field effect and electric field distortion can interfere with the dielectric constant and therefore affect the dielectric properties of the material. For model parameters and conditions for finite element simulation refer to the Supporting Information.

In summary, we fabricated a composite film consisting of CD-CuS@CNT as a filler and H-PDMS as a matrix. This composite film has an elastic modulus of below 3 MPa and can reach a maximum dielectric strength of 155.35 MV/m. Moreover, the dielectric constant and loss of the CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composite with a filler content of 2.33 vol% are 53.34 and 0.05 at 100 Hz, respectively. The enhancement of the dielectric constant is mainly attributed to the introduction of functional composite filler consisting of CD, CuS and CNT. With the high specific surface area of CD, this functional composite filler has smaller particle size and stronger interfacial polarization effect. Undeniably, this will provide a reference for further research concerning the application of carbon quantum dots in dielectric properties, while the excellent dielectric properties of CD-CuS@CNT/H-PDMS composite films will pave the way for the next generation of actuators with high actuation performance.

Ying-Jie Ma: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Jing-Wen Wang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yang Zhang: Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Zi-Long Zhang: Software, Resources. Ming-Yue Zhang: Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology. Liang Tao: Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52273247). Special thanks are extended to the Center for Microscopy and Analysis of Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics for partial testing of the data including XRD and SEM.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eurpolymj.2024.112958.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
